sentences.text: Bertha Reis.
R, like Robert, E-I-S.
Brucha Zekel.
B-R-U-C-H-A, S-- or Z.
Z-E-K-E-L.
You mean when I was young?
Well, in Polish it was the same thing.
They called me Brucha, and even in school, they called me Brucha and, and Zekel.
We didn't have, we didn't have any other names when I was young.
May the 5th, 1920.
Right.
Thank you.
Dubienka, Poland.
Well, Dubienka was a little town, small town.
There were 500 Jewish families.
I beg your pardon?
You know, close to probably 2,000 or something.
But in the middle of town, there were living-- mostly the Jewish families lived in the middle of town.
Our house was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a small street, [NON-ENGLISH].
But a big shul for Dubienka was right across the street from us and a lot of young children the same age.
We had a lot of fun.
We played.
And we did-- we went to the forest to pick berries.
We did-- we had a lot of fun when we were young.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what else?
We had a big family-- not immediate family.
Immediate, we were only four children.
And what were the names of the children?
My oldest sister was Chana.
And the youngest sister was Peshe.
And then I came in, and a brother, Ben-Zion.
My father was a carpenter, furniture maker.
In the summer he built-- he did all carpenter work in the homes-- people.
And in the winter, he made furniture, furniture for to sell, like ready-made and also furniture to order, good furniture with good polishes and stuff like that.
And he did a lot of things to support a family.
He used to also do some business.
If he heard with some partner something is going to be good this winter, so they prepared woods for everybody to buy from, from them.
And he worked very hard to make a living.
He did a lot of things.
Well, our house couldn't have any special furniture because every time somebody came to buy something they wanted only us-- ours.
So he had to throw out the bedding and sell this.
It was exactly the same as they had, as we had sold, but they thought that ours was better, so-- but for my sister, he made all the furniture.
She got married, my older sister.
He made all the furniture with the help of my cousin that helped to process the wood, just so, like, they have veneer.
And he used to stay there and work all day by hand, everything, to polish it.
Well, veneer on top, like we have here on the buffet.

There used to be over there two panels, but they had to-- it was raw, so they had to use pumice and little kind of an alcohol and stuff like that and just rub it, rub it, until it came up from a natural shine.
At the house.
We had a big place, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a barn, a big barn, to keep the furniture and to work.
They had about three, four, workers that did the furniture that was ready-made.
They took it in the markets, and people came to buy it.
But it was already made.
But special furniture he did for my sister or maybe other orders, whoever ordered it.
What?
Yeah, we were pretty comfortable.
As I say, my father worked really hard to, to save some money for the girls to give dowries.
At that time, a girl couldn't get married no matter what if you didn't have any money.
So he tried to save some money to-- so he could give dowries to the, to the daughters.
Well, I, I don't know what-- at that time, everybody was religious.
Now there's something that religious is separated.
My father was a very hard worker, went to shul twice a day to daven, left the boys working, and he went to shul in the morning.
He went to shul for mincha, maariv.
He never wore a suit.
He just wore the long, [NON-ENGLISH] or whatever you called it.
A [NON-ENGLISH] during the week.
It was a, a lighter weight coat.
And--
No.
He never wore a shtreimel.
Shtreimel were not worn in our part of the country.
Shtreimel were, were, worn in Lemberg.
In that part of the country, they wore shtreimel, and everybody wore it-- a taxi driver, everybody wore a shtreimel.
In our part of the country, they did not.
They had-- it's called a [NON-ENGLISH].
I think that's what my father's wearing on the picture.
It's just a round, like a, cap.
Yeah.
I don't remember without a wig ever.
She always wore a wig.
No.
No, she didn't.
My sister just wear, wore her own hair.
She did not wear a wig.
Well, it was very, very religious.
We children, no matter how old we were, we played outside.
We have to be, had to be home Friday when the Tata came from davening to sit down and for dinner, the Shabbos dinner.
My brother used to go with him to daven, but girls didn't.
It wasn't the style.
So we could bum around, whatever, but we had to be in the house.
We had to be for dinner Friday night.
Saturday morning, the same thing.
We had to be home for lunch when he came from shul.
And lunch was cholent from the oven.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they couldn't cook Shabbos.

They couldn't warm up anything on Shabbos, except winter we had an oven that was, had a, like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a small little oven inside, let's say, and it was put in-- the food was put in before.
So we had tea and coffee from out, from that oven that was burning from Friday.
And I thought that-- Friday night?
Friday night we had soup and whatever-- [INAUDIBLE], or whatever they, it was.
And fish, we eat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Saturday-- we never had anything that was cooked on Saturday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even our coffee was-- in the summer, our coffee was on the, in the oven, on the oven, because inside was the cholent, so it was fleishig.
So there was-- on top of the oven, they put up hot coffee and covered it up.

And it was real hot in the morning, because the oven was hot from-- so I don't think there was, that the rabbis that we had, which we had two in the city, had any kind different than every other Jew had in, in our little town.
Ours was Pesach.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my mother's was Shabbos, because Pesach she had to stay all day long and cook.
There was nothing.
We even had to make our own farfel from the matzos.
You couldn't buy it ready like nowadays.
You had to put it in in a-- I don't know what they called it, like maybe you saw a masher in the drugstores that you mash something for the medication.
We had big ones made.
My father made them.
You put in the matzos in there, and we used to do it ourselves to make farfel, to make matzo meal.
My father used to bake matzos.
This is when already, when I remember.
Otherwise, somebody else was making matzos, so we got them there.
But actually my father was baking a lot of years, I remember, until-- I guess until the war broke out.
He built a special oven, real big one, and he baked matzos for other people also.
We had a [NON-ENGLISH], which is called a [NON-ENGLISH] to bake matzos, was a [NON-ENGLISH].
And a lot of girls came to, to roll the matzos.

And you had to-- were very restrictions, like I remember that when they put in the flour, they had measurements, and if the girl that was pouring the water poured in a little bit more water, they had to roll it just the way it was.
You couldn't add any flour.
You couldn't do anything.
Just the way one time.
Just put it in one time, and they kneaded it, and then-- and they-- you also couldn't put any flour on the boards where they were rolling it.
It had to be without any flour, just the way it comes out.
This is the way they had to roll it.
If it was a little thinner, the dough, they made it a little heavier, the matzos, because they couldn't make it real thin.
It would stick to the thing.
But most of the time, it came out all right.
Sometimes they have to just throw away some dough because it was too thin.
They couldn't do it.
So you had very, a lot of restrictions that they had.
And now they also baked for people that wanted to have egg matzos, but that was considered [NON-ENGLISH] almost, but some people wanted it.
So he baked it the last night, when all matzos was finished.
They baked it the last night in the oven, and then he kashered the oven again.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
We had a Sukkah light by our window.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, everybody had a Sukkah.
There were, there were only my-- a Sukkah that you could give out, it was raining or whatever, which it most of the time, Sukkot, was raining.
You could give through the window, give out, give the food in the Sukkah, so we don't have to carry the, the food through the door.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Sukkot was a nice holiday, too, and we enjoyed it.
In, in Dubienka.
Public school, yeah.
Well, there were-- like for boys, there were cheders.
And for girls, women were teaching.
So we used to go to their house, and they were teaching to daven and whatever.
And the same thing in writing.
We had a teacher that was teaching Yiddish-- Yiddish.
And I remember since I was little, as soon as I learned something, I tried to teach somebody else.
I taught some boys Yiddish writing.
They were 14, 15, and 16 years old, and I, I must've been 10 or 12.
[LAUGHS] Because they didn't want to go to other teachers.
They didn't want to go to school.
So their mother hired me, and I used to come into their house and teach them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there was no-- I went to school a lot.
And I-- when I was 15, when I went to Lodz.
Yeah.
Everybody together.
I had a lot of non-Jewish friends when I was in school, because we didn't go to school on Shabbos.
So we really had to have good friends that we went to them Sunday to get our homework, because they wouldn't give us the homework Friday to do.
We had to go and find it out from the children.
And I had very good non-Jewish friends.
Well, I'll tell you, I really never had a Jewish accent in school.
I spoke good Polish, and the reason was because since I was little-- I was born 1920.
I must've been-- I don't know when, when we were from the-- I really wasn't in Dubienka, but like next to Dubienka.
And I was all the time with non-Jewish neighbors.
Must've been there one, one house, a Jewish house, with 100 non-Jewish houses.
And they were mixed.
I think there were Polish and Ukrainian people.
And I always had non-Jewish friends that were there, because the Jewish-- some children were older, and some like my sisters, you know, they were older.
And I think mostly the kids were there, so I had the non-Jewish friends.
And I always spoke Polish or Ukrainian, I guess Ukrainian most of the time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I came back after my father built up the houses, the home in Dubienka, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was about six years old.
And I hardly could speak Yiddish, because I spoke Yiddish, but it was like with a non-Yiddish accent.
So I remember the kids laughing at me, the way I speak.
But then it doesn't take long to get, you know, accent, but I never really had a Jewish accent in school, so I didn't have any trouble.
But other children did, because there were teachers, anti-Semites, and they used to make fun of them.
They used to say, [NON-ENGLISH].
So they, they didn't feel good.
Now, when you get older, you start reading and knowing more.
We, we felt it.
But I can't say that it was in, in our little town.
When I came to Lodz already, when I was 15, there were all kind of non-Jewish organizations, like Nazi organizations, and they used to march in the streets.
And they used to have all kinds of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] announcements.
Yeah.
In 1935 already, they used to have announcements not to buy Jews in Lodz, all over.
And they were Haller's Army, they called them, which they were Nazis.
They wore gray uniforms, and they used to march in the streets with all kind of, you know, announcements or something saying about Jews.
Well, I didn't like the small town, and I used to all the time ask my older sisters, how could they stay in such a small town?
There are no movies, no theaters, no nothing.
But I guess they didn't mind it, so-- but when I got to, you know, that age and I just figured, there's nothing to do for me there.
If you learn something to do, you just can't-- you can't use it, for money for, you know, for learning.
It was not in style girls should work in small towns-- not girls.
So the, the father had to support all the family.
So I was-- I don't know why.
That's was my-- that's the way my thinking was.
Well, one family from our, from our town moved to Lodz with the whole family.
First the daughter went there, and then she brought the whole family over.
And they assured my father that if I'll be there, they'll take care, you know.
So my-- and I needed a passport to go to, because Poland, you couldn't go any place without a passport.
So my father really had to sign for me to go, so with tears in his eyes when he was in the city hall.
So did I have.
I didn't want to leave, either.
But then something in me was that I just couldn't be.
We went to the movies.
There was one movie, and you had to sit probably four or five times.
It was tearing the film.
And it couldn't be finished, or whatever it is.
And the movie was in a, in a, somebody's-- somebody rented one room or whatever it was.
Used to be a restaurant.
And they made a movie theater there.
So I just thought there must be a better life someplace else.
And that's where I went.
And I, I really liked it.
Yes.
I first worked in a bakery for-- I used to sell.
They had a store in the front of the bakery, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I used to sell there.
And there's all of the owners of the, of that bakery, neither could write or-- read or write, I guess.
That man was almost about 75, but his wife was about 40 or 45.
He married his niece after his wife died.
So she was young, and he was old.
So he used to take me to, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to the places, to the stores.
They have bakery stuff, like now they do, too.
So they used to take it from the bakery.
And every Saturday night, we used to have to go to the Sunday-- mostly Sundays, because Sundays, the stores were closed.
The bakeries were closed.
Sunday they did not allow-- the government did not allow to have open stores.
So we used to go to the stores and get-- collect the money.
So I used to write down every day, when they picked up the bakery's stuff, and I had it in my books, and I went with the man, the owner, and collected the money from them.
So this is where I worked first.
But then I decided it's too much for me.

Saturday night, when all the kids used to have fun, go to movies, theaters, or, or there was a studio that we went to dance and to discuss things, all kind of stuff, and I had to go after Shabbos-- even before, because when it was already after Shabbos, I had to open the, the bakery store, because people used to come by.
So it was too much for me.
I wanted to live a little bit.
So I quit over there.
And I went to work-- maybe not right away, a week or two until I got a job-- sewing, so I just had to work five, six days a week.
I worked Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday.
So I had Saturday the whole day and all evening to myself.
And I could go wherever I wanted with the young kids.
I didn't have to stay and sell.
Well, I don't-- I mean, most of the kids were together, like groups.
I had a lot of friends-- a friend with a family that I really had like my second home.
They are in Cleveland.
They all were in Russia.
They survived.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] were their name, and we're still very close friends.
And they live Florida.
We come to Florida.
We get together with them.
So one of their sons was my age, and we were friends at home in Dubienka.
So all his friends, they were already in Lodz.
All his friends became my friends.
So we were all [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, we talked about everything.
We knew everything was going on in the world.
From reading-- reading papers, reading books, reading.
We knew what was going on in Russia.
We knew what was going on in Germany.
We knew every time somebody came to the government, new people in the government.
We knew that this will be trouble.
We knew all the names of, of the German people that are in government.
Well, in 1935, already, or maybe-- '35 or maybe '36, already we had refugees from Germany.
Maybe it was 1936.
There was a girl, a young girl, probably also 16 or so that came work in the place that I worked sewing.
And we knew already what they're doing in Germany to the Jews.
I don't remember when the Kristallnacht was, what year.
But I guess it was about at that time.
We knew everything.
I mean, we knew when von Papen was the president or the secretary.
We knew all the names of the people.
We knew everything.
I mean, we-- I'll tell you, I guess, young people at that time, they knew more what's going on in the world than maybe now, because it was-- it interested us with our lives that--
Well, we were young children.
We never lived through a war.
But we knew it's not good for the Jews.

We knew before the, before the elections in Germany, if Hitler is going to come to power, it's not going to be good for the Jews, because we knew what he was writing, what he was writing in his books.
So-- and then there were older people.
I'll tell you.
The man-- we had a cutter who was a very, very intelligent man.
He must've been about 50 at that time.
He had a daughter our age.
And he used to talk to us because we were about four or five workers.
And he used to say, if war broke out, breaks out, you should all run towards Russia.
